title: Die drei Männlein im Walde
titleEn: The Three Little Men in the Wood
part: The Three Little Men in the Wood
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 13. Text from Wikisource (twice proofread against the original). 1857 spelling preserved exactly.
about: The thirteenth tale in the Grimms’ collection (KHM 13), a story built on perfect mirrorings: a kind stepdaughter, sent into the winter forest for strawberries in a paper dress, shares her last crust with three little wood-goblins and is blessed — growing lovelier by the day, gold falling from her mouth at every word, and married to a king — while her spiteful stepsister, retracing her steps, earns the exact reverse: growing uglier, toads leaping from her mouth, and a wretched end. When the stepmother later drowns the young queen, she returns each night as a duck to ask after her child, until the king’s sword breaks the spell and the murderers are sealed in a nail-studded barrel and rolled into the river. Spelling follows the 1857 edition, so you will meet older forms such as daß, gieng, fieng, hieng, that, thun, Thür, Thal, Athem, heirathen and Urtheil.

sentences.t: Es war ein Mann, dem starb seine Frau, und eine Frau, der starb ihr Mann; und der Mann hatte eine Tochter, und die Frau hatte auch eine Tochter.
sentences.l: There was a man whose wife died, and a woman whose husband died; and the man had a daughter, and the woman also had a daughter.
sentences.i: There was once a man whose wife had died, and a woman whose husband had died; and the man had a daughter, and the woman had a daughter too.
sentences.n: “dem starb seine Frau” / “der starb ihr Mann” use a dative of the person affected: literally ‘to whom his wife died,’ i.e. ‘whose wife died.’ German often marks the one who suffers a loss with the dative (dem, der) rather than a possessive.

sentences.t: Die Mädchen waren mit einander bekannt und giengen zusammen spazieren und kamen hernach zu der Frau ins Haus.
sentences.l: The girls were acquainted with one another and went out walking together and came afterward to the woman’s house.
sentences.i: The girls knew one another and went walking together, and afterward came to the woman’s house.
sentences.n: “gieng” (here plural giengen) is the 1857 spelling of ging/gingen (‘went’); this -ie- spelling recurs throughout Grimm (fieng, hieng). “hernach” = afterward, an older word.

sentences.t: Da sprach sie zu des Mannes Tochter „hör, sage deinem Vater, ich wollt ihn heirathen, dann sollst du jeden Morgen dich in Milch waschen und Wein trinken, meine Tochter aber soll sich in Wasser waschen und Wasser trinken.“
sentences.l: Then she spoke to the man’s daughter, “listen, tell your father I would marry him, then you shall wash yourself in milk every morning and drink wine, but my daughter shall wash herself in water and drink water.”
sentences.i: Then she said to the man’s daughter, “Listen, tell your father I should like to marry him; then every morning you shall wash in milk and drink wine, while my daughter shall wash in water and drink water.”
sentences.n: “des Mannes Tochter” is a genitive placed before its noun, ‘the man’s daughter.’ “heirathen” is the 1857 spelling of heiraten (‘to marry’), with the old -th-. “ich wollt” = ich wollte, ‘I would (like).’

sentences.t: Das Mädchen gieng nach Haus und erzählte seinem Vater was die Frau gesagt hatte.
sentences.l: The girl went home and told its father what the woman had said.
sentences.i: The girl went home and told her father what the woman had said.
sentences.n: “Das Mädchen” is grammatically neuter, so it is taken up by “seinem” (‘its’) and later by “es” — read these as ‘her.’ German -chen/-lein nouns are all neuter, whatever the real gender.

sentences.t: Der Mann sprach „was soll ich thun? das Heirathen ist eine Freude und ist auch eine Qual.“
sentences.l: The man spoke, “what shall I do? marrying is a joy and is also a torment.”
sentences.i: The man said, “What am I to do? Marrying is a joy — and it is also a torment.”
sentences.n: “thun” is the 1857 spelling of tun (‘to do’); the old -th- runs through this edition (that, Thür, Thal). “das Heirathen” = the act of marrying (an infinitive used as a neuter noun).

sentences.t: Endlich, weil er keinen Entschluß fassen konnte, zog er seinen Stiefel aus und sagte „nimm diesen Stiefel, der hat in der Sohle ein Loch, geh damit auf den Boden, häng ihn an den großen Nagel und gieß dann Wasser hinein.
sentences.l: At last, because he could grasp no decision, he pulled off his boot and said, “take this boot, it has a hole in the sole, go up to the loft with it, hang it on the big nail and then pour water in.
sentences.i: At last, since he could come to no decision, he pulled off his boot and said, “Take this boot — it has a hole in the sole; go up to the loft with it, hang it on the big nail, and then pour water in.
sentences.n: “einen Entschluß fassen” = to reach a decision (Entschluß is the 1857 spelling of Entschluss). “zog … aus” is the separable verb ausziehen (‘to take off’); the prefix aus- jumps to the end. “Boden” here is the loft or attic.

sentences.t: Hält er das Wasser, so will ich wieder eine Frau nehmen, läufts aber durch, so will ich nicht.“
sentences.l: Holds it the water, then I will take a wife again; but runs it through, then I will not.”
sentences.i: If it holds the water, then I will take a wife again; but if it runs through, then I will not.”
sentences.n: Two conditionals made by putting the verb first: “Hält er …, so …” and “läufts … durch, so …” = ‘if it holds …, then …’ / ‘if it runs through, then …’ “läufts” = läuft es, ‘it runs’; durchlaufen is separable.

sentences.t: Das Mädchen that wie ihm geheißen war: aber das Wasser zog das Loch zusammen, und der Stiefel ward voll bis obenhin.
sentences.l: The girl did as it was bidden: but the water drew the hole together, and the boot became full up to the top.
sentences.i: The girl did as she was bidden; but the water drew the hole shut, and the boot filled right up to the brim.
sentences.n: “that” is the 1857 spelling of tat (preterite of thun/tun). “wie ihm geheißen war” = ‘as it was bidden’ (geheißen, past participle of heißen ‘to bid’). “ward” is the archaic preterite of werden (‘became’; modern wurde) — you will meet it throughout.

sentences.t: Es verkündigte seinem Vater wies ausgefallen war.
sentences.l: It announced to its father how it had turned out.
sentences.i: She told her father how it had turned out.
sentences.n: “wies” = wie es (‘how it’). “ausgefallen” is the past participle of ausfallen (‘to turn out’), a separable verb.

sentences.t: Da stieg er selbst hinauf, und als er sah daß es seine Richtigkeit hatte, gieng er zu der Wittwe und freite sie, und die Hochzeit ward gehalten.
sentences.l: Then he himself climbed up, and when he saw that it had its correctness, he went to the widow and wooed her, and the wedding was held.
sentences.i: Then he climbed up himself, and when he saw that it was all in order, he went to the widow and courted her, and the wedding was held.
sentences.n: “daß” is the 1857 spelling of dass. “seine Richtigkeit haben” is a set idiom, ‘to be all correct, in proper order.’ “freite” is the preterite of freien, an old word ‘to woo, court.’ “Wittwe” is the 1857 spelling of Witwe (‘widow’).

sentences.t: Am andern Morgen, als die beiden Mädchen sich aufmachten, da stand vor des Mannes Tochter Milch zum Waschen und Wein zum Trinken, vor der Frau Tochter aber stand Wasser zum Waschen und Wasser zum Trinken.
sentences.l: On the next morning, when the two girls got up, there stood before the man’s daughter milk for washing and wine for drinking, but before the woman’s daughter stood water for washing and water for drinking.
sentences.i: Next morning, when the two girls rose, there stood before the man’s daughter milk to wash in and wine to drink, but before the woman’s daughter stood water to wash in and water to drink.
sentences.n: “sich aufmachen” here = to set about (rising, getting ready); aufmachen is separable. “zum Waschen / zum Trinken” = ‘for washing / for drinking’ (zu + dem + infinitive used as a neuter noun).

sentences.t: Am zweiten Morgen stand Wasser zum Waschen und Wasser zum Trinken so gut vor des Mannes Tochter als vor der Frau Tochter.
sentences.l: On the second morning water for washing and water for drinking stood as well before the man’s daughter as before the woman’s daughter.
sentences.i: On the second morning, water to wash in and water to drink stood before the man’s daughter just as much as before the woman’s daughter.
sentences.n: “so gut … als” is the older form of ‘as well … as,’ where modern German would say so gut … wie.

sentences.t: Und am dritten Morgen stand Wasser zum Waschen und Wasser zum Trinken vor des Mannes Tochter, und Milch zum Waschen und Wein zum Trinken vor der Frau Tochter, und dabei bliebs.
sentences.l: And on the third morning water for washing and water for drinking stood before the man’s daughter, and milk for washing and wine for drinking before the woman’s daughter, and at that it stayed.
sentences.i: And on the third morning, water to wash in and water to drink stood before the man’s daughter, and milk to wash in and wine to drink before the woman’s daughter — and so it remained.
sentences.n: “dabei bliebs” = dabei blieb es, ‘at that it stayed’ — the good things now go to her own daughter. The threefold day-by-day pattern is a classic fairy-tale device.

sentences.t: Die Frau ward ihrer Stieftochter spinnefeind und wußte nicht wie sie es ihr von einem Tag zum andern schlimmer machen sollte.
sentences.l: The woman became bitterly hostile to her stepdaughter and did not know how she should make it worse for her from one day to the next.
sentences.i: The woman came to loathe her stepdaughter, and could not think how to make things worse for her from one day to the next.
sentences.n: “jemandem spinnefeind werden” = to become bitterly hostile to someone (spinne-feind, literally ‘spider-hostile,’ hating as one hates a spider); it takes the dative (ihrer Stieftochter). “wußte” is the 1857 spelling of wusste.

sentences.t: Auch war sie neidisch, weil ihre Stieftochter schön und lieblich war, ihre rechte Tochter aber häßlich und widerlich.
sentences.l: Also she was envious, because her stepdaughter was beautiful and lovely, but her own daughter ugly and repulsive.
sentences.i: She was envious too, for her stepdaughter was fair and lovely, while her own daughter was ugly and repulsive.
sentences.n: “rechte Tochter” = her real/own daughter (recht here = true, proper). “häßlich” is the 1857 spelling of hässlich (‘ugly’). The two girls’ looks set up the blessings and curses to come.

sentences.t: Einmal im Winter, als es steinhart gefroren hatte und Berg und Thal vollgeschneit lag, machte die Frau ein Kleid von Papier, rief das Mädchen und sprach „da zieh das Kleid an, geh hinaus in den Wald und hol mir ein Körbchen voll Erdbeeren; ich habe Verlangen danach.“
sentences.l: Once in winter, when it had frozen stone-hard and hill and dale lay snowed-full, the woman made a dress of paper, called the girl and spoke, “here, put the dress on, go out into the wood and fetch me a little basket full of strawberries; I have a craving for them.”
sentences.i: Once in winter, when everything had frozen hard as stone and hill and dale lay deep under snow, the woman made a dress out of paper, called the girl and said, “Here, put this dress on, go out into the forest, and fetch me a little basket of strawberries; I have a craving for them.”
sentences.n: “steinhart gefroren” = frozen hard as stone; “vollgeschneit” = snowed over, buried in snow. “zieh … an” is the separable verb anziehen (‘to put on’); the imperative zieh stays up front and an goes to the end. Sending her for strawberries in deep winter is meant as an impossible, deadly errand.

sentences.t: „Du lieber Gott,“ sagte das Mädchen, „im Winter wachsen ja keine Erdbeeren, die Erde ist gefroren, und der Schnee hat auch alles zugedeckt.
sentences.l: “You dear God,” said the girl, “in winter no strawberries grow, the earth is frozen, and the snow has covered everything up too.
sentences.i: “Good heavens,” said the girl, “no strawberries grow in winter — the ground is frozen, and the snow has covered everything over as well.
sentences.n: “Du lieber Gott” is an exclamation, ‘Good heavens.’ “ja” here is a flavouring particle underlining the obvious (‘after all’). “zugedeckt” is the past participle of zudecken (‘to cover up’), separable.

sentences.t: Und warum soll ich in dem Papierkleide gehen? es ist draußen so kalt, daß einem der Athem friert: da weht ja der Wind hindurch und die Dornen reißen mirs vom Leib.“
sentences.l: And why should I go in the paper dress? it is so cold outside that one’s breath freezes: for the wind blows right through it and the thorns tear it off my body.”
sentences.i: And why must I go in the paper dress? It is so cold outside that your very breath freezes; the wind blows straight through it, and the thorns tear it off my body.”
sentences.n: “einem der Athem friert” — the dative einem = ‘for one, for you’: ‘the breath freezes on one.’ “Athem” is the 1857 spelling of Atem (‘breath’). “mirs” = mir es (‘it … off me’); “vom Leib reißen” = to tear off (the) body.

sentences.t: „Willst du mir noch widersprechen?“ sagte die Stiefmutter, „mach daß du fortkommst, und laß dich nicht eher wieder sehen als bis du das Körbchen voll Erdbeeren hast.“
sentences.l: “Do you still want to contradict me?” said the stepmother, “see that you get away, and don’t let yourself be seen again before you have the little basket full of strawberries.”
sentences.i: “Do you mean to talk back to me?” said the stepmother. “Be off with you, and don’t let yourself be seen again until you have the little basket full of strawberries.”
sentences.n: “mach daß du fortkommst” = literally ‘make that you get away,’ i.e. ‘be off, get going’; fortkommen is separable. “nicht eher … als bis” = ‘not sooner … than until,’ i.e. ‘not until.’

sentences.t: Dann gab sie ihm noch ein Stückchen hartes Brot und sprach „davon kannst du den Tag über essen,“ und dachte „draußen wirds erfrieren und verhungern und mir nimmermehr wieder vor die Augen kommen.“
sentences.l: Then she gave it besides a little piece of hard bread and spoke, “on that you can eat the day through,” and thought, “out there it will freeze and starve and nevermore come before my eyes again.”
sentences.i: Then she gave her a little piece of hard bread besides, and said, “You can live on that through the day,” and thought, “Out there she will freeze and starve, and never come before my eyes again.”
sentences.n: “wirds” = wird es (‘it will’). “erfrieren” = to freeze to death, “verhungern” = to starve to death — she means the errand to kill the girl. “nimmermehr” = nevermore. “jemandem vor die Augen kommen” = to come into someone’s sight.

sentences.t: Nun war das Mädchen gehorsam, that das Papierkleid an und gieng mit dem Körbchen hinaus.
sentences.l: Now the girl was obedient, put the paper dress on and went out with the little basket.
sentences.i: Now the girl was obedient; she put on the paper dress and went out with the little basket.
sentences.n: “that … an” is the separable verb anthun/antun, here in its literal sense ‘put on’ (paired with a garment); “that” is the old preterite of thun. Later anthun appears in its other sense, ‘to do (harm) to.’

sentences.t: Da war nichts als Schnee die Weite und Breite, und war kein grünes Hälmchen zu merken.
sentences.l: There was nothing but snow the length and breadth, and not a green little blade was to be perceived.
sentences.i: There was nothing but snow far and wide, and not a single green blade of grass to be seen.
sentences.n: “die Weite und Breite” = ‘the width and breadth,’ i.e. far and wide (an accusative of extent). “zu merken” = to be perceived, noticed (sein + zu + infinitive expresses possibility, ‘to be seen’).

sentences.t: Als es in den Wald kam, sah es ein kleines Häuschen, daraus guckten drei kleine Haulemännerchen.
sentences.l: When it came into the wood, it saw a little house, out of which three little goblin-men peered.
sentences.i: When she came into the forest, she saw a little house, out of which three little goblins were peeping.
sentences.n: “daraus” = ‘out of which.’ “guckten” (gucken) = to peep, peer. The Haulemännerchen are little woodland goblins or hill-sprites (a dialect word), left with their own name; they play the fairy-tale role of the ‘helpers by the road’ who reward courtesy.

sentences.t: Es wünschte ihnen die Tageszeit und klopfte bescheidenlich an die Thür.
sentences.l: It wished them the time of day and knocked modestly at the door.
sentences.i: She wished them good day and knocked modestly at the door.
sentences.n: “jemandem die Tageszeit wünschen” = to bid someone good day (a set courtesy). “bescheidenlich” is an archaic adverb, ‘modestly, humbly’ (modern bescheiden). “Thür” is the 1857 spelling of Tür. Her politeness is exactly what earns her reward.

sentences.t: Sie riefen herein, und es trat in die Stube und setzte sich auf die Bank am Ofen, da wollte es sich wärmen und sein Frühstück essen.
sentences.l: They called ‘come in,’ and it stepped into the room and sat down on the bench by the stove; there it wanted to warm itself and eat its breakfast.
sentences.i: They called out ‘come in,’ and she stepped into the room and sat down on the bench by the stove, meaning to warm herself and eat her breakfast.
sentences.n: “riefen herein” = called ‘(come) in’ (hereinrufen). “trat” is the preterite of treten (‘to step’). “sich wärmen” = to warm oneself (reflexive).

sentences.t: Die Haulemännerchen sprachen „gib uns auch etwas davon.“
sentences.l: The little goblins spoke, “give us some of it too.”
sentences.n: “gib” is the imperative of geben. “davon” = ‘of it.’ Sharing her scanty bread is the test — and she passes it.

sentences.t: „Gerne“ sprach es, theilte sein Stückchen Brot entzwei und gab ihnen die Hälfte.
sentences.l: “Gladly,” it spoke, divided its little piece of bread in two and gave them the half.
sentences.i: “Gladly,” she said, broke her little piece of bread in two, and gave them half.
sentences.n: “theilte” is the 1857 spelling of teilte (teilen, ‘to divide, share’); the old -th- recurs. “entzwei” = in two, apart.

sentences.t: Sie fragten „was willst du zur Winterzeit in deinem dünnen Kleidchen hier im Wald?“
sentences.l: They asked, “what do you want in wintertime in your thin little dress here in the wood?”
sentences.i: They asked, “What are you after in wintertime, in your thin little dress, here in the forest?”
sentences.n: “was willst du … hier” = ‘what do you want here,’ i.e. what are you doing here. “Kleidchen” is the diminutive of Kleid (‘little dress’).

sentences.t: „Ach,“ antwortete es, „ich soll ein Körbchen voll Erdbeeren suchen, und darf nicht eher nach Hause kommen als bis ich es mitbringe.“
sentences.l: “Oh,” it answered, “I am to look for a little basket full of strawberries, and may not come home before I bring it.”
sentences.i: “Oh,” she answered, “I’m to find a little basket of strawberries, and I may not come home until I bring it.”
sentences.n: “ich soll” = I am (supposed) to; “ich darf nicht” = I am not allowed to. “nicht eher … als bis” again = ‘not until.’ “mitbringe” (mitbringen) is separable.

sentences.t: Als es sein Brot gegessen hatte, gaben sie ihm einen Besen und sprachen „kehre damit an der Hinterthüre den Schnee weg.“
sentences.l: When it had eaten its bread, they gave it a broom and spoke, “sweep the snow away with it at the back door.”
sentences.i: When she had eaten her bread, they gave her a broom and said, “Sweep the snow away with this at the back door.”
sentences.n: “kehre … weg” is the separable verb wegkehren (‘to sweep away’); the imperative kehre stays up front and weg goes to the end. “Hinterthüre” = back door (Hinter- + Thüre).

sentences.t: Wie es aber draußen war, sprachen die drei Männerchen untereinander „was sollen wir ihm schenken, weil es so artig und gut ist und sein Brot mit uns getheilt hat?“
sentences.l: But when it was outside, the three little men spoke among themselves, “what shall we give it, since it is so well-behaved and good and has shared its bread with us?”
sentences.i: But when she was outside, the three little men said among themselves, “What shall we give her, since she is so well-behaved and good, and has shared her bread with us?”
sentences.n: “was sollen wir ihm schenken” = ‘what shall we give it (as a gift).’ “artig” = well-behaved, good (of a child). “getheilt” is the 1857 spelling of geteilt. This begins the threefold blessing that will later be inverted by the curse.

sentences.t: Da sagte der erste „ich schenk ihm daß es jeden Tag schöner wird.“
sentences.l: Then the first said, “I give it as a gift that it grows more beautiful every day.”
sentences.i: Then the first said, “My gift to her is that she shall grow more beautiful every day.”
sentences.n: “ich schenk ihm daß …” = ‘I bestow on her (the gift) that …’ — the little men grant blessings by decreeing them. This is the first of three: beauty. Watch how each will be exactly reversed for the wicked sister.

sentences.t: Der zweite sprach „ich schenk ihm daß Goldstücke ihm aus dem Mund fallen, so oft es ein Wort spricht.“
sentences.l: The second spoke, “I give it as a gift that gold pieces fall from its mouth, as often as it speaks a word.”
sentences.i: The second said, “My gift to her is that gold pieces shall fall from her mouth every time she speaks a word.”
sentences.n: The gold-from-the-mouth blessing: her very words turn to gold. “so oft” = as often as, every time. This reward-motif will be mirrored by the wicked sister’s toads — good speech made precious, spiteful speech made loathsome.

sentences.t: Der dritte sprach „ich schenk ihm daß ein König kommt und es zu seiner Gemahlin nimmt.“
sentences.l: The third spoke, “I give it as a gift that a king comes and takes it for his wife.”
sentences.i: The third said, “My gift to her is that a king shall come and take her for his wife.”
sentences.n: “zu seiner Gemahlin nehmen” = to take as one’s wife (Gemahlin = a lofty word for ‘wife, consort’). The third gift — marriage to a king — completes the reward and drives the second half of the tale.

sentences.t: Das Mädchen aber that wie die Haulemännerchen gesagt hatten, kehrte mit dem Besen den Schnee hinter dem kleinen Hause weg, und was glaubt ihr wohl daß es gefunden hat?
sentences.l: But the girl did as the little goblins had said, swept the snow away with the broom behind the little house, and what do you suppose it found?
sentences.i: But the girl did as the little goblins had said, swept the snow away with the broom behind the little house — and what do you suppose she found?
sentences.n: The narrator turns to address you directly: “was glaubt ihr wohl daß es gefunden hat?” = ‘what do you suppose she found?’ (wohl softens it to ‘I wonder’). “kehrte … weg” (wegkehren) is separable.

sentences.t: lauter reife Erdbeeren, die ganz dunkelroth aus dem Schnee hervor kamen.
sentences.l: nothing but ripe strawberries, which came forth quite dark-red out of the snow.
sentences.i: nothing but ripe strawberries, all dark red, peeping out of the snow.
sentences.n: “lauter” = nothing but, sheer. “dunkelroth” is the 1857 spelling of dunkelrot. “hervor kamen” (hervorkommen, to come forth) is separable. The impossible winter strawberries are the sign that the gift has taken hold.

sentences.t: Da raffte es in seiner Freude sein Körbchen voll, dankte den kleinen Männern, gab jedem die Hand und lief nach Haus, und wollte der Stiefmutter das Verlangte bringen.
sentences.l: Then in its joy it snatched its little basket full, thanked the little men, gave each its hand and ran home, and wanted to bring the stepmother what she had asked for.
sentences.i: Then in her joy she gathered her little basket full, thanked the little men, shook hands with each, and ran home, meaning to bring the stepmother what she had asked for.
sentences.n: “raffte … voll” = snatched/gathered (it) full (raffen, to snatch up hastily). “jedem die Hand geben” = to shake hands with each. “das Verlangte” = ‘the thing demanded,’ a participle used as a noun.

sentences.t: Wie es eintrat und „guten Abend“ sagte, fiel ihm gleich ein Goldstück aus dem Mund.
sentences.l: As it stepped in and said “good evening,” at once a gold piece fell from its mouth.
sentences.i: As she stepped in and said “good evening,” a gold piece fell at once from her mouth.
sentences.n: “eintrat” (eintreten, to step in, enter) is separable. Here the second gift shows itself: her first words drop gold. “gleich” = at once, straight away.

sentences.t: Darauf erzählte es was ihm im Walde begegnet war, aber bei jedem Worte, das es sprach, fielen ihm die Goldstücke aus dem Mund, so daß bald die ganze Stube damit bedeckt ward.
sentences.l: Thereupon it told what had happened to it in the wood, but with every word that it spoke, the gold pieces fell from its mouth, so that soon the whole room was covered with them.
sentences.i: Then she told what had befallen her in the forest, but with every word she spoke, the gold pieces fell from her mouth, so that soon the whole room was strewn with them.
sentences.n: “begegnen” takes the dative (ihm), ‘to happen to someone.’ “Walde” and “Worte” carry the old dative -e (Wald, Wort). “ward” again — the archaic preterite of werden, here forming the passive ‘was covered.’

sentences.t: „Nun sehe einer den Übermuth,“ rief die Stiefschwester, „das Geld so hinzuwerfen,“ aber heimlich war sie neidisch darüber und wollte auch hinaus in den Wald und Erdbeeren suchen.
sentences.l: “Now just let someone look at the insolence,” cried the stepsister, “to throw the money about like that,” but secretly she was envious of it and wanted to go out into the wood too and look for strawberries.
sentences.i: “Just look at the airs she gives herself,” cried the stepsister, “throwing money about like that!” — but secretly she was envious of it, and she too wanted to go out into the forest and look for strawberries.
sentences.n: “nun sehe einer den Übermuth” = ‘now let someone look at the insolence’ (sehe is a wishing/imperative subjunctive; einer = ‘someone’). “Übermuth” is the 1857 spelling of Übermut (‘high-handedness’). “hinzuwerfen” (hinwerfen) is separable.

sentences.t: Die Mutter: „nein, mein liebes Töchterchen, es ist zu kalt, du könntest mir erfrieren.“
sentences.l: The mother: “no, my dear little daughter, it is too cold, you could freeze to death on me.”
sentences.i: The mother said: “No, my dear little daughter, it is too cold — you might freeze to death.”
sentences.n: “du könntest mir erfrieren” — könntest is subjunctive II (‘you might/could’); the dative “mir” is a dative of concern, ‘on me, to my sorrow.’ Note the mother’s tenderness to her own child, the mirror of her cruelty to the stepdaughter.

sentences.t: Weil sie ihr aber keine Ruhe ließ, gab sie endlich nach, nähte ihm einen prächtigen Pelzrock, den es anziehen mußte, und gab ihm Butterbrot und Kuchen mit auf den Weg.
sentences.l: But because she gave her no peace, she at last gave in, sewed it a splendid fur coat, which it had to put on, and gave it bread-and-butter and cake along on the way.
sentences.i: But since she gave her no peace, she gave in at last, sewed her a splendid fur coat, which she had to put on, and gave her bread and butter and cake to take along.
sentences.n: “gab … nach” (nachgeben, to give in) is separable. “Pelzrock” = fur coat. Note the lavish outfitting — the exact opposite of the paper dress — a contrast the tale draws pointedly.

sentences.t: Das Mädchen gieng in den Wald und gerade auf das kleine Häuschen zu.
sentences.l: The girl went into the wood and straight up to the little house.
sentences.i: The girl went into the forest and straight up to the little house.
sentences.n: “gerade auf … zu” = straight toward; zugehen auf (to go up to) is separable, and here the zu sits at the end. This is now the wicked sister, retracing her stepsister’s path — but everything she does will be the reverse.

sentences.t: Die drei kleinen Haulemänner guckten wieder, aber es grüßte sie nicht, und, ohne sich nach ihnen umzusehen und ohne sie zu grüßen, stolperte es in die Stube hinein, setzte sich an den Ofen und fieng an sein Butterbrot und seinen Kuchen zu essen.
sentences.l: The three little goblins peered again, but it did not greet them, and, without looking round at them and without greeting them, it stumbled into the room, sat down by the stove and began to eat its bread-and-butter and its cake.
sentences.i: The three little goblins peeped out again, but she did not greet them, and, without so much as glancing at them or bidding them good day, she stumbled into the room, sat down by the stove, and began to eat her bread and butter and her cake.
sentences.n: “ohne … umzusehen” = without looking round (sich umsehen, reflexive + separable, here as a zu-infinitive). “fieng an” is the 1857 spelling of fing an (anfangen, ‘to begin’), separable. Her rudeness — the exact reverse of her stepsister’s courtesy — seals her fate.

sentences.t: „Gib uns etwas davon,“ riefen die Kleinen, aber es antwortete „es schickt mir selber nicht, wie kann ich andern noch davon abgeben?“
sentences.l: “Give us some of it,” cried the little ones, but it answered, “it does not befit me myself, how can I give away any of it to others?”
sentences.i: “Give us some of it,” cried the little ones, but she answered, “There’s scarcely enough for me — how can I give any of it away to others?”
sentences.n: “es schickt mir … nicht” = ‘it does not befit / is not fitting for me,’ i.e. there is scarcely enough for herself. “abgeben” (to give away, hand over a share) is separable. Where her sister divided her one crust, this girl will not share her feast.

sentences.t: Als es nun fertig war mit dem Essen, sprachen sie „da hast du einen Besen, kehr uns draußen vor der Hinterthür rein.“
sentences.l: When it was now finished with the eating, they spoke, “there you have a broom, sweep it clean for us outside before the back door.”
sentences.i: When she had now finished eating, they said, “There’s a broom for you — sweep the ground clean for us outside the back door.”
sentences.n: “kehr … rein” = sweep (it) clean (kehren + rein, colloquial ‘clean’); the imperative kehr stays up front. “Hinterthür” = back door.

sentences.t: „Ei, kehrt euch selber,“ antwortete es, „ich bin eure Magd nicht.“
sentences.l: “Bah, sweep for yourselves,” it answered, “I am not your maidservant.”
sentences.i: “Bah, sweep it yourselves,” she answered, “I am not your servant-girl.”
sentences.n: “Ei” is a dismissive exclamation, ‘bah, pooh.’ “kehrt euch selber” = ‘sweep (for) yourselves’ (a 2nd-person plural imperative). “Magd” = maidservant. Her insolence is the third and final failure of courtesy.

sentences.t: Wie es sah daß sie ihm nichts schenken wollten, gieng es zur Thüre hinaus.
sentences.l: When it saw that they would give it nothing, it went out the door.
sentences.i: When she saw that they meant to give her nothing, she went out the door.
sentences.n: “zur Thüre hinaus” = out through the door (hinausgehen, separable, ‘to go out’). She leaves exactly as her sister did — but with the opposite reception waiting.

sentences.t: Da sprachen die kleinen Männer untereinander „was sollen wir ihm schenken, weil es so unartig ist und ein böses neidisches Herz hat, das niemand etwas gönnt?“
sentences.l: Then the little men spoke among themselves, “what shall we give it, since it is so ill-behaved and has an evil, envious heart that grants no one anything?”
sentences.i: Then the little men said among themselves, “What shall we give her, since she is so naughty and has an evil, envious heart that cannot bear anyone to have anything?”
sentences.n: “unartig” = naughty, ill-behaved (the opposite of artig above). “gönnen” = to grant, wish someone the good they have; “niemand etwas gönnen” = to begrudge everyone everything. The little men frame the curse to fit the fault, as they framed the blessing to fit the virtue.

sentences.t: Der erste sprach „ich schenk ihm daß es jeden Tag häßlicher wird.“
sentences.l: The first spoke, “I give it as a gift that it grows uglier every day.”
sentences.i: The first said, “My gift to her is that she shall grow uglier every day.”
sentences.n: The exact inversion of the first blessing (schöner, ‘more beautiful’): here häßlicher, ‘uglier.’ The parallel wording — “ich schenk ihm daß …” — makes the mirroring unmistakable.

sentences.t: Der zweite sprach „ich schenk ihm daß ihm bei jedem Wort, das es spricht, eine Kröte aus dem Munde springt.“
sentences.l: The second spoke, “I give it as a gift that, at every word it speaks, a toad springs out of its mouth.”
sentences.i: The second said, “My gift to her is that, at every word she speaks, a toad shall leap out of her mouth.”
sentences.n: The mirror of the gold-from-the-mouth blessing: where the good sister’s words drop gold, the spiteful sister’s drop toads (Kröte). “Munde” has the old dative -e (Mund). Grimm does not soften the punishment — the toads are literal and repellent.

sentences.t: Der dritte sprach „ich schenk ihm daß es eines unglücklichen Todes stirbt.“
sentences.l: The third spoke, “I give it as a gift that it dies an unhappy death.”
sentences.i: The third said, “My gift to her is that she shall die a wretched death.”
sentences.n: “eines unglücklichen Todes sterben” = to die an unlucky/wretched death — a genitive of the ‘kind of death’ died (an old construction, like English ‘to die a natural death’). This third curse mirrors the third blessing, marriage to a king, with its grim opposite.

sentences.t: Das Mädchen suchte draußen nach Erdbeeren, als es aber keine fand, gieng es verdrießlich nach Haus.
sentences.l: The girl looked outside for strawberries, but when it found none, it went home sullenly.
sentences.i: The girl looked for strawberries outside, but when she found none, she went home sulking.
sentences.n: “nach etwas suchen” = to search for something. “verdrießlich” = sullen, cross, out of sorts. No sweeping, no blessing, no berries — the reward turns on the sharing she refused.

sentences.t: Und wie es den Mund aufthat und seiner Mutter erzählen wollte was ihm im Walde begegnet war, da sprang ihm bei jedem Wort eine Kröte aus dem Mund, so daß alle einen Abscheu vor ihm bekamen.
sentences.l: And when it opened its mouth and wanted to tell its mother what had happened to it in the wood, then at every word a toad sprang from its mouth, so that all conceived a disgust for it.
sentences.i: And when she opened her mouth and tried to tell her mother what had befallen her in the forest, at every word a toad leapt from her mouth, so that they all came to loathe her.
sentences.n: “aufthat” is the old preterite of aufthun/auftun (‘to open’), separable. “einen Abscheu vor jemandem bekommen” = to come to feel disgust for someone. The curse now works exactly as the blessing did — speech made the visible measure of the heart.

sentences.t: Nun ärgerte sich die Stiefmutter noch viel mehr und dachte nur darauf wie sie der Tochter des Mannes alles Herzeleid anthun wollte, deren Schönheit doch alle Tage größer ward.
sentences.l: Now the stepmother was vexed still much more and thought only on how she might do the man’s daughter every heart-sorrow, whose beauty grew greater every day.
sentences.i: Now the stepmother was more vexed than ever, and thought only how she might work every heartbreak upon the man’s daughter, whose beauty grew greater with every passing day.
sentences.n: “sich ärgern” = to be vexed (reflexive). “jemandem Herzeleid anthun” = to inflict heart’s-grief on someone; anthun (1857 -th-, modern antun) is separable and takes a dative person. “deren” = ‘whose’ (feminine relative), pointing back to the daughter.

sentences.t: Endlich nahm sie einen Kessel, setzte ihn zum Feuer und sott Garn darin.
sentences.l: At last she took a kettle, set it to the fire and boiled yarn in it.
sentences.i: At last she took a cauldron, set it on the fire, and boiled yarn in it.
sentences.n: “sott” is the preterite of sieden (‘to boil, seethe’) — an old strong verb (past participle gesotten, next). Boiling the yarn is a pretext to send the girl onto the frozen river.

sentences.t: Als es gesotten war, hieng sie es dem armen Mädchen auf die Schulter, und gab ihm eine Axt dazu, damit sollte es auf den gefrornen Fluß gehen, ein Eisloch hauen und das Garn schlittern.
sentences.l: When it was boiled, she hung it on the poor girl’s shoulder and gave it an axe besides; with these it was to go onto the frozen river, hew an ice-hole and rinse the yarn.
sentences.i: When it was boiled, she hung it on the poor girl’s shoulder and gave her an axe as well; with these she was to go out onto the frozen river, hew an ice-hole, and rinse the yarn.
sentences.n: “hieng” is the 1857 spelling of hing (hängen). “gesotten” is the past participle of sieden (‘boiled’). “schlittern” here = to rinse/swill the yarn in the ice-hole; “gefrornen” = frozen (a syncopated form of gefrorenen). Sending a barely-clad girl onto river ice is another attempt on her life.

sentences.t: Es war gehorsam, gieng hin und hackte ein Loch in das Eis, und als es mitten im Hacken war, kam ein prächtiger Wagen hergefahren, worin der König saß.
sentences.l: It was obedient, went there and hacked a hole in the ice, and when it was in the midst of hacking, a splendid carriage came driving up, in which the king sat.
sentences.i: She was obedient, went out and hacked a hole in the ice; and while she was in the midst of the hacking, a splendid carriage came driving up, in which the king was sitting.
sentences.n: “gieng hin” (hingehen) is separable. “mitten im Hacken” = in the middle of (the) hacking (an infinitive used as a noun). “kam … hergefahren” = came driving up (herfahren, separable; the past participle after kommen gives the manner of arrival).

sentences.t: Der Wagen hielt still und der König fragte „mein Kind, wer bist du und was machst du da?“
sentences.l: The carriage held still and the king asked, “my child, who are you and what are you doing there?”
sentences.i: The carriage stopped, and the king asked, “My child, who are you, and what are you doing there?”
sentences.n: “hielt still” (stillhalten, to hold still, stop) is separable. Simple, direct dialogue — the king’s pity begins here.

sentences.t: „Ich bin ein armes Mädchen und schlittere Garn.“
sentences.l: “I am a poor girl and am rinsing yarn.”
sentences.i: “I am a poor girl, rinsing yarn.”
sentences.n: “schlittere” = 1st-person singular of schlittern, here ‘to rinse/swill yarn on the ice.’

sentences.t: Da fühlte der König Mitleiden, und als er sah wie es so gar schön war, sprach er „willst du mit mir fahren?“
sentences.l: Then the king felt compassion, and when he saw how it was so very beautiful, he spoke, “will you ride with me?”
sentences.i: Then the king felt pity, and when he saw how very beautiful she was, he said, “Will you ride away with me?”
sentences.n: “Mitleiden” = compassion, pity (an older form of Mitleid). “gar” here intensifies: “so gar schön” = ‘so very beautiful.’ Her beauty — the first little man’s gift — is what wins the king, just as decreed.

sentences.t: „Ach ja, von Herzen gern,“ antwortete es, denn es war froh daß es der Mutter und Schwester aus den Augen kommen sollte.
sentences.l: “Oh yes, gladly from the heart,” it answered, for it was glad that it should come out of the mother’s and sister’s eyes.
sentences.i: “Oh yes, with all my heart,” she answered, for she was glad to get out of the sight of her stepmother and sister.
sentences.n: “von Herzen gern” = gladly, with all one’s heart (a set phrase). “jemandem aus den Augen kommen” = to get out of someone’s sight. The third gift — the king — now comes true.

sentences.t: Also stieg es in den Wagen und fuhr mit dem König fort, und als sie auf sein Schloß gekommen waren, ward die Hochzeit mit großer Pracht gefeiert, wie es die kleinen Männlein dem Mädchen geschenkt hatten.
sentences.l: So it climbed into the carriage and drove off with the king, and when they had come to his castle, the wedding was celebrated with great splendour, as the little men had bestowed on the girl.
sentences.i: So she climbed into the carriage and drove off with the king, and when they had reached his castle, the wedding was celebrated with great splendour, just as the little men had promised the girl.
sentences.n: “fuhr … fort” (fortfahren, to drive off) is separable. “Männlein” = little men (diminutive of Mann). “ward … gefeiert” — the archaic ward forms the passive ‘was celebrated.’ All three gifts are now fulfilled.

sentences.t: Über ein Jahr gebar die junge Königin einen Sohn, und als die Stiefmutter von dem großen Glücke gehört hatte, so kam sie mit ihrer Tochter in das Schloß und that als wollte sie einen Besuch machen.
sentences.l: After a year the young queen bore a son, and when the stepmother had heard of the great good fortune, she came with her daughter to the castle and acted as if she wanted to pay a visit.
sentences.i: A year on, the young queen bore a son; and when the stepmother had heard of her great good fortune, she came with her daughter to the castle and pretended she had come to pay a visit.
sentences.n: “über ein Jahr” = after a year, a year later. “gebar” is the preterite of gebären (‘to bear, give birth to’). “that als wollte sie …” = ‘acted as if she wanted to …’ (that = old preterite of thun; als + subjunctive wollte for ‘as if’). “Glücke” has the old dative -e.

sentences.t: Als aber der König einmal hinausgegangen und sonst niemand zugegen war, packte das böse Weib die Königin am Kopf, und ihre Tochter packte sie an den Füßen, hoben sie aus dem Bett und warfen sie zum Fenster hinaus in den vorbei fließenden Strom.
sentences.l: But when the king had once gone out and no one else was present, the wicked woman seized the queen by the head, and her daughter seized her by the feet, lifted her out of the bed and threw her out the window into the river flowing past.
sentences.i: But once, when the king had gone out and no one else was present, the wicked woman seized the queen by the head, and her daughter seized her by the feet, and they lifted her out of the bed and flung her out of the window into the river flowing past below.
sentences.n: “hinausgegangen” (hinausgehen, to go out) is separable. “zugegen sein” = to be present. “Weib” here = woman (older, now coarse). “vorbei fließenden” = flowing past (vorbeifließen). The drowning is left brutally plain, as the tale intends.

sentences.t: Darauf legte sich ihre häßliche Tochter ins Bett, und die Alte deckte sie zu bis über den Kopf.
sentences.l: Thereupon her ugly daughter laid herself in the bed, and the old woman covered her up over the head.
sentences.i: Then her ugly daughter lay down in the bed, and the old woman covered her up over her head.
sentences.n: “deckte … zu” (zudecken, to cover up) is separable. “die Alte” = the old woman (an adjective used as a noun). The imposture: the ugly daughter is passed off as the queen in the bed.

sentences.t: Als der König wieder zurück kam und mit seiner Frau sprechen wollte, rief die Alte „still, still, jetzt geht das nicht, sie liegt in starkem Schweiß, ihr müßt sie heute ruhen lassen.“
sentences.l: When the king came back again and wanted to speak with his wife, the old woman cried, “hush, hush, it won’t do just now, she lies in a heavy sweat, you must let her rest today.”
sentences.i: When the king came back and wanted to speak with his wife, the old woman cried, “Hush, hush, not now — she is in a heavy sweat; you must let her rest today.”
sentences.n: “zurück kam” (zurückkommen) is separable. “das geht nicht” = ‘that won’t do.’ “ihr müßt” = ‘you must’ (polite/plural ihr; müßt is the 1857 spelling of müsst). The old woman’s lie buys time for the imposture.

sentences.t: Der König dachte nichts Böses dabei und kam erst den andern Morgen wieder, und wie er mit seiner Frau sprach, und sie ihm Antwort gab, sprang bei jedem Wort eine Kröte hervor, während sonst ein Goldstück heraus gefallen war.
sentences.l: The king thought nothing evil by it and came again only the next morning, and when he spoke with his wife, and she gave him answer, at every word a toad sprang out, whereas otherwise a gold piece had fallen out.
sentences.i: The king suspected nothing amiss and did not come again until the next morning; and when he spoke with his wife, and she answered him, at every word a toad leapt out, whereas before a gold piece had always fallen out.
sentences.n: “nichts Böses dabei denken” = to think nothing of it, suspect no harm. “erst den andern Morgen” = only the next morning. The two mouth-gifts now betray the fraud: toads (the curse) where gold (the blessing) used to fall — the motif that runs through the whole tale.

sentences.t: Da fragte er was das wäre, aber die Alte sprach das hätte sie von dem starken Schweiß gekriegt, und würde sich schon wieder verlieren.
sentences.l: Then he asked what that might be, but the old woman said she had got that from the heavy sweat, and it would surely pass off again.
sentences.i: Then he asked what this meant, but the old woman said she had caught it from the heavy sweat, and it would soon pass off again.
sentences.n: Reported speech in the subjunctive: “was das wäre … das hätte sie … gekriegt … würde sich … verlieren” — German marks the old woman’s account (as claimed, not as fact) with the Konjunktiv, a pattern you meet constantly in Grimm. “gekriegt” (kriegen) = got, caught (colloquial). “sich verlieren” = to fade, pass off.

sentences.t: In der Nacht aber sah der Küchenjunge wie eine Ente durch die Gosse geschwommen kam, die sprach
sentences.l: But in the night the kitchen-boy saw how a duck came swimming through the gutter, which spoke
sentences.i: But in the night the kitchen boy saw a duck come swimming up through the gutter, and it spoke:
sentences.n: “geschwommen kam” = came swimming (schwimmen; the past participle after kommen gives the manner of arrival). “Gosse” = gutter, drain. The murdered queen returns as a duck — the sentence breaks off, and the duck’s verse question follows.

sentences.t:
„König, was machst du?
schläfst du oder wachst du?“
sentences.l:
“King, what are you doing?
are you asleep or awake?”
sentences.i:
“King, what do you do?
Do you sleep, or do you wake?”
sentences.n: The duck’s nightly refrain, in rhyme (machst du / wachst du). “wachst du” is from wachen (‘to be awake, to wake’), not wachsen (‘to grow’). Each night the drowned, transformed queen comes to ask after the king, her guests, and her child.

sentences.t: Und als er keine Antwort gab, sprach sie
sentences.l: And when he gave no answer, she spoke

sentences.t: „was machen meine Gäste?“
sentences.l: “what are my guests doing?”
sentences.n: The second question of the refrain — after the king, she asks after her ‘guests,’ the household. Note the rhyme it sets up with the reply (Gäste / feste).

sentences.t: Da antwortete der Küchenjunge
sentences.l: Then the kitchen-boy answered

sentences.t: „sie schlafen feste.“
sentences.l: “they sleep fast.”
sentences.i: “They are sound asleep.”
sentences.n: “feste” is an emphatic/older form of fest, ‘fast, soundly’; it rhymes with Gäste.

sentences.t: Fragte sie weiter
sentences.l: She asked further
sentences.i: She asked on:

sentences.t: „was macht mein Kindelein?“
sentences.l: “what is my little child doing?”
sentences.i: “What does my little child do?”
sentences.n: “Kindelein” = little child (an old diminutive of Kind). The third and tenderest question — after her baby; it rhymes with the reply (Kindelein / fein).

sentences.t: Antwortete er
sentences.l: He answered

sentences.t: „es schläft in der Wiege fein.“
sentences.l: “it sleeps fine in the cradle.”
sentences.i: “It sleeps sweetly in its cradle.”
sentences.n: “fein” here = nicely, sweetly, and rhymes with Kindelein. “Wiege” = cradle. The whole exchange is a little sung dialogue, question and answer in couplets.

sentences.t: Da gieng sie in der Königin Gestalt hinauf, gab ihm zu trinken, schüttelte ihm sein Bettchen, deckte es zu und schwamm als Ente wieder durch die Gosse fort.
sentences.l: Then she went up in the queen’s shape, gave it to drink, shook up its little bed, covered it and swam off again as a duck through the gutter.
sentences.i: Then she went up in the queen’s own shape, gave the child drink, shook up its little bed, tucked it in, and swam off again as a duck through the gutter.
sentences.n: “in der Königin Gestalt” = in the queen’s shape (a genitive before its noun). “ihm … sein Bettchen” refers to the child (das Kind, neuter): ‘its little bed.’ “schwamm … fort” (fortschwimmen) is separable. Each night she resumes human form to tend her baby, then turns back into the duck.

sentences.t: So kam sie zwei Nächte, in der dritten sprach sie zu dem Küchenjungen „geh und sage dem König daß er sein Schwert nimmt und auf der Schwelle dreimal über mir schwingt.“
sentences.l: Thus she came two nights; on the third she spoke to the kitchen-boy, “go and tell the king that he takes his sword and swings it three times over me on the threshold.”
sentences.i: Thus she came for two nights; on the third she said to the kitchen boy, “Go and tell the king to take his sword and swing it over me three times on the threshold.”
sentences.n: The fairy-tale third time brings the release. “Schwelle” = threshold; “schwingt” (schwingen) = swings, brandishes. The disenchantment: the sword swung thrice over the shape breaks the spell.

sentences.t: Da lief der Küchenjunge und sagte es dem König, der kam mit seinem Schwert und schwang es dreimal über dem Geist: und beim drittenmal stand seine Gemahlin vor ihm, frisch, lebendig und gesund, wie sie vorher gewesen war.
sentences.l: Then the kitchen-boy ran and told it to the king, who came with his sword and swung it three times over the spectre: and at the third time his wife stood before him, fresh, alive and well, as she had been before.
sentences.i: Then the kitchen boy ran and told the king, who came with his sword and swung it three times over the apparition; and at the third stroke his wife stood before him, fresh, alive and well, as she had been before.
sentences.n: “der kam” — the demonstrative der (‘and he’) resumes the king. “Geist” here = the spirit-shape, apparition. “beim drittenmal” = at the third time. The queen is restored whole — the spell undone at the ritual third stroke.

sentences.t: Nun war der König in großer Freude, er hielt aber die Königin in einer Kammer verborgen bis auf den Sonntag, wo das Kind getauft werden sollte.
sentences.l: Now the king was in great joy, but he kept the queen hidden in a chamber until the Sunday, when the child was to be baptized.
sentences.i: Now the king was overjoyed, but he kept the queen hidden in a chamber until the Sunday when the child was to be christened.
sentences.n: “verborgen halten” = to keep hidden (verborgen, past participle of verbergen). “bis auf den Sonntag” = until (and including) the Sunday. “getauft werden sollte” = was to be baptized (a passive with sollen).

sentences.t: Und als es getauft war, sprach er „was gehört einem Menschen, der den andern aus dem Bett trägt und ins Wasser wirft?“
sentences.l: And when it was baptized, he spoke, “what belongs to a person who carries another out of the bed and throws them into the water?”
sentences.i: And when it was christened, he said, “What does a person deserve who carries another out of bed and throws them into the water?”
sentences.n: “was gehört einem Menschen …?” = ‘what is due, coming to a person …?’ (gehören + dative). The king, not yet naming her, leads the old woman into pronouncing her own sentence — the folktale trick of self-condemnation.

sentences.t: „Nichts besseres,“ antwortete die Alte, „als daß man den Bösewicht in ein Faß steckt, das mit Nägeln ausgeschlagen ist, und den Berg hinab ins Wasser rollt.“
sentences.l: “Nothing better,” answered the old woman, “than that one puts the villain into a barrel that is studded with nails, and rolls him down the hill into the water.”
sentences.i: “Nothing better,” answered the old woman, “than to put the villain into a barrel studded with nails, and roll him down the hill into the water.”
sentences.n: “Bösewicht” = villain, evildoer. “mit Nägeln ausgeschlagen” = lined/studded with nails (ausschlagen, here of fitting out the inside). “den Berg hinab” = down the hill. She unknowingly names her own punishment — the very death she dealt out returns on her.

sentences.t: Da sagte der König „du hast dein Urtheil gesprochen,“ ließ ein solches Faß holen und die Alte mit ihrer Tochter hineinstecken, dann ward der Boden zugehämmert und das Faß bergab gekullert, bis es in den Fluß rollte.
sentences.l: Then the king said, “you have spoken your own sentence,” had such a barrel fetched and the old woman with her daughter put into it; then the bottom was hammered shut and the barrel rolled downhill, until it rolled into the river.
sentences.i: Then the king said, “You have pronounced your own sentence,” and had such a barrel fetched and the old woman and her daughter put into it; then the bottom was hammered shut and the barrel trundled downhill until it rolled into the river.
sentences.n: “dein Urtheil sprechen” = to pronounce one’s (own) judgment (Urtheil, 1857 spelling of Urteil). “ließ … holen … hineinstecken” = had (it) fetched and (them) put in (lassen + infinitive, the causative ‘have something done’). “ward … zugehämmert” — archaic ward forms the passive; zuhämmern = to hammer shut. “gekullert” (kullern) = rolled, trundled. The punishment mirrors the crime exactly.
